arguments.task: Open official Atlassian first-party pages for Jira developer research. Read live page content, not snippets. Find canonical pages that document: (1) Jira Cloud platform REST API overview and auth methods, including OAuth 2.0 and any documented API token or Basic auth usage; (2) Jira pricing or plans page showing free/standard/premium or other self-serve tiers; (3) any GraphQL API page if Atlassian documents one for Jira/Atlassian platform; (4) any official Atlassian MCP server or remote MCP server documentation. Return a concise evidence-backed summary with exact canonical URLs read, one-line Jira description, auth methods documented, access tier conclusion, API surface type and breadth, whether MCP exists, buildability verdict, rate limit notes, and notable signals. If a structured field cannot be supported from official pages actually opened, say so explicitly.

input.thought: Use the browser to inspect official Atlassian documentation and pricing pages directly for Jira research evidence.

raw_result.description: Jira is a project management and issue-tracking platform for software teams with APIs for issues, projects, workflows, and related Atlassian data.
raw_result.auth_notes: Official Atlassian docs show Jira Cloud REST uses OAuth 2.0 (3LO), API tokens, and Basic auth with email plus API token; docs also reference app-specific auth via Forge/Atlassian Connect, which is mapped to other.
raw_result.gating_reason: Official Jira pricing lists a Free plan alongside Standard, Premium, and Enterprise, so developers can self-serve with a free tier.

api_surface.breadth: Jira has a broad Jira Cloud REST API for issues, projects, users, workflows, and configuration, and Atlassian also documents the Atlassian GraphQL Gateway for cross-product queries that includes Jira data.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Atlassian documents an official cloud-hosted Atlassian Rovo MCP server at mcp.atlassian.com/v1/mcp for accessing Jira and other Atlassian products.

raw_result.rate_limit_notes: The gathered evidence explicitly mentions GraphQL cost-based limits of 10,000 points per minute; the browser summary also notes REST uses per-app/per-user limits, but exact REST limit details were not verified from a dedicated rate-limit page in this pass.
raw_result.notable_signals: Atlassian exposes multiple integration surfaces for Jira—REST, GraphQL, Forge/Connect app auth, and an official MCP server—showing strong support for AI-agent and developer integrations.

result.description: Jira is a project management and issue-tracking platform for software teams with APIs for issues, projects, workflows, and related Atlassian data.
result.auth_notes: Official Atlassian docs show Jira Cloud REST uses OAuth 2.0 (3LO), API tokens, and Basic auth with email plus API token; docs also reference app-specific auth via Forge/Atlassian Connect, which is mapped to other.
result.gating_reason: Official Jira pricing lists a Free plan alongside Standard, Premium, and Enterprise, so developers can self-serve with a free tier.

api_surface.breadth: Jira has a broad Jira Cloud REST API for issues, projects, users, workflows, and configuration, and Atlassian also documents the Atlassian GraphQL Gateway for cross-product queries that includes Jira data.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Atlassian documents an official cloud-hosted Atlassian Rovo MCP server at mcp.atlassian.com/v1/mcp for accessing Jira and other Atlassian products.

result.rate_limit_notes: The gathered evidence explicitly mentions GraphQL cost-based limits of 10,000 points per minute; the browser summary also notes REST uses per-app/per-user limits, but exact REST limit details were not verified from a dedicated rate-limit page in this pass.
result.notable_signals: Atlassian exposes multiple integration surfaces for Jira—REST, GraphQL, Forge/Connect app auth, and an official MCP server—showing strong support for AI-agent and developer integrations.
